era_context: 1195 CE falls within the late 12th century, a period of significant political fragmentation and cultural flourishing across Eurasia. The Ayyubid succession crisis following Saladin's death (1193) was ongoing. The Angevin Empire under Richard I was under pressure from Philip II Augustus. The Khmer Empire under Jayavarman VII was at its territorial peak. Japan's Kamakura shogunate was consolidating power. The Byzantine Empire was in decline, facing internal instability and external pressure from Crusaders and Turks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary records)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Canute VI of Denmark dies; succession of Valdemar II
events.description: King Canute VI of Denmark died on 12 November 1191 (not 1195, but his succession triggered events that dominated the 1190s). His son Valdemar II (Valdemar the Conqueror) succeeded him and would become one of Denmark's greatest medieval kings. By 1195, Valdemar II was consolidating power and beginning the military campaigns that would expand Danish territory and influence in the Baltic and North Sea. Valdemar's reign (1191–1241) marked the height of Danish medieval power. This succession is attested in Scandinavian chronicles and later historiographic synthesis.
key_figures: Valdemar II of Denmark (King, known as 'the Conqueror')
Archbishop Absalon of Lund (advisor and military leader)

sources.name: Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (History of the Danes), compiled early 13th century

events.certainty_note: Valdemar II's accession is well-attested, but the specific dating of events in 1195 relies on later compilations. Saxo Grammaticus wrote in the early 13th century, introducing potential chronological distortion.
cross_references: 1191-001 (Canute VI's death and Valdemar II's accession)

events.title: Hubert Walter appointed Archbishop of Canterbury; English ecclesiastical reform
events.description: Hubert Walter (c. 1140–1205), a prominent English cleric and administrator, was appointed Archbishop of Canterbury in 1193 and was actively engaged in ecclesiastical and political affairs in 1195. He served as justiciar (chief administrator) under Richard I and later King John, making him one of the most powerful figures in England. Walter was instrumental in raising Richard's ransom (1194) and in managing English affairs during the king's absences. His tenure as archbishop was marked by efforts to reform the English church and strengthen ecclesiastical authority. Walter's career exemplifies the intersection of religious and secular power in medieval England.
key_figures: Hubert Walter (Archbishop of Canterbury, Justiciar of England)
events.certainty_note: Hubert Walter's appointment and activities are well-documented in contemporary English chronicles. His role in 1195 is attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Byzantine civil war; Andronikos II Palaiologos and Alexios III conflict
events.description: The Byzantine Empire was in turmoil in the 1190s. Emperor Andronikos II Palaiologos (r. 1282–1328) had not yet come to power, but the empire was ruled by Alexios III Angelos (r. 1195–1203), who seized power in a coup in 1195. Alexios III deposed and blinded his brother Isaac II Angelos, consolidating power through military force. This internal instability weakened the empire's ability to resist external threats from Crusaders, Turks, and Balkan states. The coup is attested in Byzantine chronicles and later historiographic accounts. This period marks the beginning of the end for the Byzantine Empire's control of the eastern Mediterranean.
key_figures: Euphrosyne Doukaina Kamatera (empress, wife of Alexios III)

sources.name: Niketas Choniates, History (Historia), written early 13th century

events.certainty_note: Alexios III's coup in 1195 is well-documented in Byzantine sources. The date and basic facts are confirmed, though interpretations of the causes and consequences vary.

events.title: Ghurid Empire expansion in India; Muhammad of Ghor campaigns
events.description: Muhammad of Ghor (Muhammad Ghori, c. 1149–1206), ruler of the Ghurid Empire, was actively expanding his control over northern India in the 1190s. In 1192, he defeated the Rajput confederation at the Second Battle of Tarain, opening the way for Islamic conquest of northern India. By 1195, Muhammad of Ghor was consolidating his gains and establishing the foundations of the Delhi Sultanate, which would dominate northern India for centuries. His military campaigns and administrative innovations marked a turning point in Indian history, transitioning from Hindu-dominated kingdoms to Islamic sultanates. The Ghurid expansion is attested in Islamic chronicles and later Indian historical sources.
key_figures: Muhammad of Ghor (Ghurid ruler, military conqueror)
Qutb ud-Din Aibak (Muhammad's general, founder of Delhi Sultanate)

sources.name: Taj-ul-Maasir by Hasan Nizami, compiled early 13th century
Firishta, History of the Muhammadan Empire in India, compiled 16th century

events.certainty_note: Muhammad of Ghor's military campaigns are attested in Islamic sources, but precise dating of events in 1195 is uncertain. Most sources were compiled centuries after the events, introducing potential chronological distortion.
cross_references: 1206-001 (Muhammad of Ghor's death; establishment of Delhi Sultanate)

events.title: Angkor Wat temple complex in use; Khmer religious and administrative centre
events.description: Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world, was in active use as a Hindu temple (and later Buddhist temple) in 1195. Built in the early 12th century under King Suryavarman II, Angkor Wat served as both a religious centre and a symbol of Khmer power and cosmological understanding. By the late 12th century, under Jayavarman VII, the temple was being incorporated into a broader Buddhist religious and administrative complex. The temple's bas-reliefs, architecture, and inscriptions provide evidence of Khmer religious practices, military organisation, and daily life. Angkor Wat remains one of the most significant archaeological sites in Southeast Asia.
key_figures: Jayavarman VII (Khmer king, patron of Buddhist temples)
Suryavarman II (earlier builder of Angkor Wat, r. 1113–1150)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Angkor Wat temple complex
Epigraphic evidence from Khmer inscriptions at Angkor Wat
Zhou Daguan, Record of Cambodia (Zhenla Fengtu Ji), written 1297

events.certainty_note: Angkor Wat's existence and use in 1195 are confirmed by archaeological evidence and epigraphic records. The temple's religious and administrative functions are well-attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Alexios III's coup (1195-003) is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward usurpation; however, modern scholarship suggests that Isaac II's mental and physical decline made the succession crisis more complex, and Alexios III's initial support from the aristocracy was broader than simple usurpation narratives suggest. The dating of Muhammad of Ghor's campaigns (1195-004) is uncertain; some sources place key events in 1192, others in 1195 or later. The precise chronology of Ghurid expansion is debated among historians. Valdemar II's early reign (1195-001) is sometimes portrayed as immediately triumphant, but modern scholarship emphasises that his consolidation of power took several years and faced significant opposition.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1195 is moderate, with good coverage of Europe, the Islamic world, and East Asia, but relying heavily on later compilations. European sources are predominantly Latin and Norman; Islamic sources are primarily Arabic chronicles written decades or centuries later. Byzantine sources are from Niketas Choniates, written in the early 13th century, introducing chronological uncertainty. Indian sources are almost entirely from later compilations (13th century or later), making precise dating difficult. Khmer sources are primarily archaeological and epigraphic. The record is shaped by the interests of literate elites and may omit significant events in non-literate communities. Scandinavian sources are particularly sparse for this period, relying on Saxo Grammaticus's later compilation.

graph_edges.note: Valdemar II's consolidation of power in 1195 followed his accession in 1191.
Alexios III's internal instability contributed to the Byzantine Empire's vulnerability to the Fourth Crusade in 1203.
Muhammad of Ghor's consolidation in 1195 was part of his broader conquest of northern India following the Second Battle of Tarain.
Angkor Wat's continued use in 1195 was part of its long history as a religious and administrative centre.
